reason.Occupational Certificate: Chef (QCTO, NQ: An NQF 5 Chef certificate is the safest baseline for skilled hospitality visas and VETASSESS assessment.
reason.National Certificate: Professional Cooke: A CATHSSETA cookery certificate is a solid start but may need topping up to NQF 5 for skilled visa routes.
reason.Diploma in Culinary Arts / Hotel Managem: A culinary or hotel-management diploma is well regarded by Gulf hotel groups and supports skilled-visa applications.
reason.Degree in Hospitality / Culinary Arts: A hospitality or culinary degree is a strong credential that opens senior and management routes abroad.
reason.In-house training only — no formal quali: On-the-job training alone limits skilled-visa options; a recognised qualification would widen your routes.
reason.None: No formal qualification is a significant blocker for skilled-visa routes; consider an NQF 5 Chef certificate first.

reason.Yes — current: A current trade test certificate meets the VETASSESS Pathway 1 requirement and strengthens Gulf hotel applications.
reason.In progress: Your trade test in progress; completing it unlocks the Australian VETASSESS route.
reason.No: No trade test certificate; this is a hard requirement for the Australian skilled route and helps UAE 5-star applications.
reason.Not sure: Confirm your trade test status, as it is decisive for the Australian skilled route.

reason.Head Chef / Executive Chef: Senior chefs are in acute global shortage and open the strongest routes, including the Australian skilled visa and Gulf 5-star hotels.
reason.Sous Chef / Senior Chef de Partie: Senior chef roles are in acute demand and qualify for the Australian skilled visa and Gulf hotel recruitment.
reason.Chef de Partie / Line Chef: Line chefs are widely recruited by Gulf hotels and cruise lines; very strong demand abroad.
reason.Commis Chef / Junior Cook: Junior kitchen roles hire steadily abroad, though they offer fewer skilled-visa pathways than senior chef posts.
reason.Pastry / Bakery: Pastry specialists are scarce and heavily recruited by Gulf 5-star hotels and cruise lines.
reason.Restaurant / F&B Manager: F&B and restaurant management is in solid demand at Gulf hotels, though more competitive than senior kitchen roles.
reason.Bartender / Mixologist: Skilled bartenders are recruited by Gulf hotels and cruise lines, though competition is higher than for chefs.
reason.Hotel Reception / Concierge: Front-office roles hire at Gulf hotels and on cruise lines, but demand is steadier rather than acute.
reason.Waiter / Front of House: Front-of-house roles are more competitive and often seasonal; pairing with a qualification or 5-star experience helps you stand out.
reason.Housekeeping: Housekeeping roles are widely available but competitive and lower-paid; cruise lines and Gulf hotels are your best route.
reason.Kitchen Porter / Steward: Steward roles are entry-level and competitive; consider upskilling toward a commis or chef role to widen your options.
reason.Other: Confirm how your role maps to in-demand hospitality jobs abroad, as demand varies sharply by position.

ranges.reason: Under 2 years experience; most Gulf 5-star hotels and skilled routes expect at least 2 years.
2 to 5 years experience meets the bar for most Gulf hotel and cruise-line roles.
5 to 8 years experience is a strong profile for senior posts and skilled-visa routes.
8+ years experience marks you as a senior, highly employable candidate abroad.

reason.UAE / Dubai: Prior experience in the UAE is a major advantage; Gulf hotel groups favour candidates who know the market.
reason.UK: Prior UK hospitality experience strengthens applications and signals international standards.
reason.Saudi Arabia / Qatar / Bahrain: Prior Gulf experience is a strong advantage for hotel-group recruitment.
reason.Australia: Prior Australian experience supports skilled-visa applications and signals international standards.
reason.Canada: Prior Canadian experience strengthens your profile for international employers.
reason.Cruise ship: Prior cruise-ship experience is highly valued and reopens cruise-line and hotel routes quickly.
reason.Other country: Prior international experience improves applications; agencies favour candidates who have worked abroad.
reason.None: No overseas experience yet; this is workable, but Gulf hotels and cruise lines favour returners.

rules.empty_reason: No prior overseas hospitality experience recorded. Any international experience improves applications, so add it if you have any.

reason.Valid — 18+ months remaining: Passport valid, meets typical work-visa requirements.
reason.Valid — under 18 months remaining: Passport valid but renew soon; most work visas need a longer validity window.
reason.Expired: Passport expired; renew before applying abroad.
reason.No passport: No passport; apply immediately, as this is the first blocker.

reason.Current (within 6 months): Current SAPS police clearance, meets the visa-ready bar.
reason.Older than 6 months: Police clearance older than 6 months; most visa applications require a fresh one.
reason.Applied — waiting: Police clearance applied for; keep the receipt handy while SAPS processes it.
reason.None: No police clearance; apply now, as SAPS turnaround is typically 4 to 8 weeks.

reason.Under R10k: Below the typical pre-departure budget; the UAE route is most accessible as employers usually cover costs.
reason.R10k – R30k: Covers a UK-style move but tight for the Australian skilled route's upfront fees.
reason.R30k – R60k: A comfortable budget for most hospitality routes other than the costlier Australian skilled visa.
reason.R60k+: A strong financial position that covers even the upfront Australian VETASSESS and visa costs.
reason.Prefer not to say: Budget not declared; share it privately with us if it shapes your destination options.

reason.Native / first language: English as a first language clears the language bar for UK, Gulf, and Australian routes.
reason.Fluent: Fluent English is typically sufficient for most hospitality destinations.
reason.Conversational: Conversational English works for many kitchen roles, but skilled visas often require a formal test.
reason.Basic: Basic English is a real blocker for guest-facing and skilled-visa roles abroad.

reason.Have valid IELTS/OET/PTE (within 2 years: A valid English test on record meets the requirement for the Australian skilled route and most employers.
reason.Booked / studying for one: English test booked; sit it early, as the Australian skilled route requires the result.
reason.Not yet — need to take one: No English test yet; the Australian skilled route and some employers will require one.
reason.Not applicable to my target: No test needed for your target route; confirm this with the employer before applying.

reason.true: Bringing dependants adds visa steps and cost; UAE work visas often don't cover dependants below a salary threshold.
reason.false: No dependants to relocate, the easiest profile to move quickly.

reason.2026: A 2026 departure means you should start applications and documents now to match recruitment cycles.
reason.2027: A 2027 horizon is realistic for most routes once documents and any trade test are ready.
reason.2028: A 2028 horizon gives ample time for credential preparation and visa processing.
reason.Just exploring: Exploring only, so no urgency; you can plan your route strategically.
